approaches.name: Brute Force O(n²)
approaches.summary: For each bar, compute trapped water by finding the maximum height to its left and right, then take the minimum of these minus the bar's height. This involves scanning left and right for every bar, making it inefficient for large inputs.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Brute Force Trapping Rain Water
ladders.desc: Complete O(n²) solution computing trapped water by scanning left/right max for each bar
ladders.explanation:
The brute force approach solves the trapping rain water problem by examining each bar individually to determine how much water it can hold. The key insight is that water trapped above a bar is determined by the minimum of the maximum heights to its left and right, minus the bar's own height. For each bar at index i, we scan leftwards from i-1 to 0 to find the maximum height (leftMax), then scan rightwards from i+1 to n-1 to find the maximum height (rightMax). The water trapped at bar i is min(leftMax, rightMax) - height[i], but only if this value is positive (otherwise it's 0). We sum this value across all bars to get total trapped water.

Intuition: Water accumulates above a bar when there are taller bars on both sides that can 'contain' it. The water level at any point is limited by the shorter of the two containing walls (left and right maxima), similar to how water in a U-shaped container settles at the height of the lower side. The bar's own height subtracts from this because water can only sit above the bar, not below it.

Algorithm:
1. Initialize totalWater = 0
2. For each index i from 0 to n-1:
   a. Find leftMax = maximum height in heights[0..i-1] (scan left)
   b. Find rightMax = maximum height in heights[i+1..n-1] (scan right)
   c. waterAtI = min(leftMax, rightMax) - height[i]
   d. If waterAtI > 0, add to totalWater
3. Return totalWater

Time Complexity: O(n²) because for each of n bars, we scan up to n bars to find left/right maxima (worst-case scanning n-1 elements each time). Space Complexity: O(1) as we only use a few variables.

Connection to L1: This full solution repeatedly calls the 'find maximum in subarray' operation that L1 focuses on, applying it twice per bar.

Common Mistakes:
1. Forgetting to handle edge cases where leftMax or rightMax don't exist (when i=0 or i=n-1) - in these cases, water trapped is 0.
2. Not checking if waterAtI is positive before adding - negative values would incorrectly reduce total water.
3. Including the current bar in left/right scans - must exclude height[i] itself.

Real Code Pattern:
def trap_water_brute(heights):
    n = len(heights)
    total = 0
    for i in range(n):
        left_max = 0
        for j in range(i-1, -1, -1):
            left_max = max(left_max, heights[j])
        right_max = 0
        for j in range(i+1, n):
            right_max = max(right_max, heights[j])
        water = min(left_max, right_max) - heights[i]
        if water > 0:
            total += water
    return total

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Finding Maximum in Subarray
ladders.desc: Scanning a subarray to find maximum height, excluding current element
ladders.explanation:
This sub-routine is the core computational unit of the brute force approach. For each bar in the trapping rain water problem, we need to find the maximum height to its left (in indices 0 to i-1) and to its right (in indices i+1 to n-1). This involves scanning through portions of the array and tracking the maximum value encountered.

Intuition: The maximum height in a subarray represents the tallest 'wall' that could contain water from that direction. When looking left from a bar, we want to know the highest barrier that would prevent water from flowing leftwards. Similarly for the right side. By finding these maxima separately, we can determine the limiting height for water accumulation at that position.

Algorithm for left maximum:
1. Initialize maxHeight = 0 (or -∞ if heights can be 0)
2. For index j from i-1 down to 0:
   a. Update maxHeight = max(maxHeight, heights[j])
3. Return maxHeight

For right maximum, same but scanning from i+1 to n-1.

Time Complexity: O(k) where k is the size of the subarray being scanned. In worst case (scanning nearly entire array), this is O(n). Space Complexity: O(1).

Connection to L0: This sub-routine is called twice for each bar in the full solution - once for left scan, once for right scan. The O(n²) complexity arises because we perform O(n) work for each of n bars.

Connection to L2: This operation implements the 'boundary finding' concept where we identify the tallest barriers on each side.

Common Mistakes:
1. Including the current element in the scan - must start from i-1 (not i) for left scan and i+1 (not i) for right scan.
2. Not handling empty subarrays (when i=0 for left scan or i=n-1 for right scan) - should return 0 or appropriate base value.
3. Using inefficient data structures - simple linear scan is correct for this brute force approach.

Real Code Pattern:
def find_max_in_subarray(heights, start, end, step):
    max_val = 0
    for i in range(start, end, step):
        if heights[i] > max_val:
            max_val = heights[i]
    return max_val

# For left max: find_max_in_subarray(heights, i-1, -1, -1)
# For right max: find_max_in_subarray(heights, i+1, len(heights), 1)

ladders.title: Core Logic — Water Calculation per Bar
ladders.desc: Computing water trapped at a single bar given left/right maxima
ladders.explanation:
This core logic determines how much water accumulates above a single bar once we know the maximum heights to its left and right. The formula is: water = min(leftMax, rightMax) - height[i], but only if this value is positive (otherwise water = 0). This calculation embodies the physical principle that water trapped above a bar is limited by the shorter of the two containing walls.

Intuition: Imagine pouring water into a terrain shaped by the bars. At any point, water will rise until it reaches the height of the lower barrier on either side, then overflow. The bar's own height acts as the floor - water can only sit above it. So the water depth is the lower barrier height minus the floor height. If the bar itself is taller than the lower barrier, no water accumulates there.

Step-by-step:
1. Input: bar height h, left maximum L, right maximum R
2. Find limiting height = min(L, R)
3. Compute potential water = limiting height - h
4. If potential water > 0, that's the actual water
5. Otherwise, water = 0

Time Complexity: O(1) for a single calculation. Space Complexity: O(1).

Connection to L1: This logic uses the leftMax and rightMax values found by the subarray scanning routine from L1.

Connection to L0: The full solution applies this calculation to every bar and sums the results.

Common Mistakes:
1. Using max(L,R) instead of min(L,R) - water is limited by the shorter wall, not the taller one.
2. Forgetting to check for negative results - if the bar is taller than the limiting height, subtraction gives negative but water should be 0.
3. Not handling cases where L or R is 0 (at edges) - min(0,anything)=0, so water calculation correctly gives 0 or negative.

Real Code Pattern:
def water_at_bar(height, left_max, right_max):
    limiting_height = min(left_max, right_max)
    water = limiting_height - height
    return water if water > 0 else 0

ladders.title: Building Block — Finding Minimum of Two Values
ladders.desc: Determining the smaller of two numbers, a fundamental comparison operation
ladders.explanation:
This building block focuses on finding the minimum of two values, which is crucial for the water calculation logic. In the trapping rain water problem, the water level at any bar is limited by the shorter of the left and right maximum heights. The min() operation captures this limiting factor.

Intuition: When water is contained between two walls, it cannot rise higher than the shorter wall - it would overflow. So to determine how high water can rise at a point, we need to know which side provides the lower constraint. This is analogous to the classic 'bucket effect' where a bucket holds water only up to its lowest side.

Algorithm:
1. Input: two numbers a and b
2. If a < b, return a
3. Otherwise, return b

Or using built-in min() function.

Time Complexity: O(1). Space Complexity: O(1).

Connection to L2: This min() operation is used in the water calculation formula: limiting_height = min(leftMax, rightMax).

Connection to L4: This builds upon basic comparison operations (less-than, greater-than).

Common Mistakes:
1. Using max() instead of min() - easy confusion but critically different.
2. Not considering equal values - min(x,x) should return x.
3. Overcomplicating with unnecessary conditional branches.

Real Code Pattern:
def minimum(a, b):
    return a if a < b else b

# Or simply:
limiting_height = min(left_max, right_max)

3 < 5? True
Therefore min(5,3) = 3

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Element Comparison and Maximum Tracking
ladders.desc: Comparing array elements and updating a running maximum
ladders.explanation:
This basic operation involves scanning through elements and maintaining the maximum value seen so far. It's fundamental to finding the leftMax and rightMax for each bar in the brute force solution. The operation requires comparing the current element with the current maximum and updating if the element is larger.

Intuition: As we examine elements sequentially, we want to remember the tallest one encountered. This is like looking at a series of buildings and keeping mental note of the tallest seen so far. Each new building might be taller than all previous ones, requiring an update to our mental maximum.

Algorithm for tracking maximum in a scan:
1. Initialize max_so_far = 0 (or first element)
2. For each element x in the sequence:
   a. If x > max_so_far, update max_so_far = x
   b. Otherwise, keep max_so_far unchanged
3. After scanning all elements, max_so_far contains the maximum

Time Complexity: O(n) for scanning n elements. Space Complexity: O(1).

Connection to L3: This maximum tracking is used to find leftMax and rightMax, which are then fed into the min() operation.

Connection to L1: This is the core of the 'find maximum in subarray' sub-routine.

Common Mistakes:
1. Initializing max_so_far incorrectly - should be 0 or negative infinity if heights can be 0.
2. Using < instead of > in comparison - would track minimum instead of maximum.
3. Not updating max_so_far when values are equal - equal values don't change the maximum.

Real Code Pattern:
max_val = 0
for height in heights:
    if height > max_val:
        max_val = height
# max_val now contains maximum

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Array Indexing and Looping
ladders.desc: Accessing array elements by index and iterating through sequences
ladders.explanation:
This foundational concept covers basic array operations: accessing elements by their index position and looping through arrays. In the trapping rain water problem, we need to examine each bar (array element) and also scan left/right subarrays. Understanding zero-based indexing and loop constructs is essential.

Intuition: An array is like a row of boxes numbered starting from 0. Each box contains a value (bar height). To work with the array, we need to know how to look at specific boxes (indexing) and how to examine boxes in sequence (looping).

Key operations:
1. Accessing element at index i: heights[i]
2. Looping through all indices: for i in range(len(heights)):
3. Looping backwards: for i in range(start, end, -1):

For the brute force solution, we use:
- Forward loops to process each bar
- Backward loops to scan left from a bar
- Forward loops to scan right from a bar

Time Complexity: Accessing by index is O(1). Looping through n elements is O(n).

Connection to L4: These looping constructs enable the maximum tracking operation that scans through array elements.

Connection to L0: The full solution uses nested loops: outer loop for each bar, inner loops for left/right scans.

Common Mistakes:
1. Off-by-one errors: range(n) goes 0 to n-1, not 0 to n.
2. Confusing index with value: heights[i] gives the value at position i, not the index itself.
3. Incorrect loop bounds when scanning subarrays.

Real Code Pattern:
# Access element
height = heights[2]  # Gets third element

# Loop forward
for i in range(len(heights)):
    print(heights[i])

# Loop backward from index 4 to 0
for i in range(4, -1, -1):
    print(heights[i])

approaches.name: Dynamic Programming O(n)
approaches.summary: Precompute the maximum height to the left and right for each bar using two passes to store results in arrays. Then, compute trapped water in a third pass using these precomputed values, avoiding repeated scans.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Trapping Rain Water with Dynamic Programming
ladders.desc: Complete solution using two-pass DP to precompute left/right max heights, then calculate trapped water.
ladders.explanation: The intuition behind this approach is that for any bar at position i, the water it can trap is determined by the minimum of the maximum heights to its left and right, minus its own height. The key insight is that we can precompute these maximum heights efficiently using dynamic programming instead of scanning repeatedly for each bar. For left_max[i], we store the highest bar from index 0 to i-1. For right_max[i], we store the highest bar from i+1 to n-1. We compute left_max in one forward pass: left_max[i] = max(left_max[i-1], height[i-1]). We compute right_max in one backward pass: right_max[i] = max(right_max[i+1], height[i+1]). Then in a third pass, for each i, water = min(left_max[i], right_max[i]) - height[i], if positive. Time complexity is O(n) for three passes, space is O(n) for two arrays. This connects to L1 by using the key subroutine of computing trapped water at each position. Common mistakes include not handling negative water values (use max with 0), off-by-one errors in array indices, and misunderstanding that water is trapped by surrounding higher bars, not just adjacent ones. Real code pattern: def trap(height): n = len(height); if n < 3: return 0; left_max = [0]*n; right_max = [0]*n; for i in range(1,n): left_max[i] = max(left_max[i-1], height[i-1]); for i in range(n-2,-1,-1): right_max[i] = max(right_max[i+1], height[i+1]); water = 0; for i in range(n): water += max(0, min(left_max[i], right_max[i]) - height[i]); return water.

Compute right_max: right_max[5]=0; i=4: right_max[4]=max(0,5)=5; i=3: right_max[3]=max(5,2)=5; i=2: right_max[2]=max(5,3)=5; i=1: right_max[1]=max(5,0)=5; i=0: right_max[0]=max(5,2)=5. Right_max: [5,5,5,5,5,0]. Compute water: i=0: min(0,5)-4=-4→0; i=1: min(4,5)-2=2; i=2: min(4,5)-0=4; i=3: min(4,5)-3=1; i=4: min(4,5)-2=2; i=5: min(4,0)-5=-5→0. Sum: 0+2+4+1+2+0=9.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Computing Trapped Water at Each Position
ladders.desc: Calculate water trapped at a specific bar given left and right maximum heights.
ladders.explanation: The intuition is that for any bar, the water above it is limited by the shorter of the highest bars to its left and right, because water will overflow from the lower side. The height of water at position i is min(left_max, right_max) - height[i], but only if this value is positive; otherwise, no water is trapped (e.g., if the bar is taller than the limiting side). This sub-routine is the core calculation used in the full problem after precomputing left_max and right_max arrays. Step-by-step: 1. Input: height[i], left_max[i], right_max[i]. 2. Compute limiting_height = min(left_max[i], right_max[i]). 3. Compute water_height = limiting_height - height[i]. 4. If water_height > 0, return water_height; else return 0. Time complexity is O(1) per bar, space is O(1). This connects to L0 by being the final step in the three-pass algorithm. Common mistakes include forgetting to take the minimum (using max instead), not checking for negative values, and misunderstanding that left_max and right_max exclude the current bar's height. Real code pattern: def water_at_i(height_i, left_max_i, right_max_i): return max(0, min(left_max_i, right_max_i) - height_i). This sub-problem can be coded independently by providing the three values.

Step 1: limiting_height = min(3,5) = 3. Step 2: water_height = 3 - 4 = -1. Step 3: Since -1 <= 0, return 0 (no water trapped as bar is taller than left max).

ladders.title: Core Logic — Precomputing Left and Right Maximum Heights
ladders.desc: Compute arrays of maximum heights to the left and right for each position using DP.
ladders.explanation: The intuition is that to efficiently find the maximum height to the left of each bar, we can use a cumulative approach: left_max[i] is the maximum of left_max[i-1] and height[i-1], since we already know the max up to i-1. Similarly, right_max[i] is the maximum of right_max[i+1] and height[i+1] when traversing backwards. This avoids O(n²) scanning for each bar. Step-by-step for left_max: Initialize left_max[0] = 0 (no bars to left of first). For i from 1 to n-1: left_max[i] = max(left_max[i-1], height[i-1]). For right_max: Initialize right_max[n-1] = 0 (no bars to right of last). For i from n-2 down to 0: right_max[i] = max(right_max[i+1], height[i+1]). Time complexity is O(n) for each pass, space is O(n) for the arrays. This connects to L1 by providing the inputs needed for water calculation. Common mistakes include including the current bar's height in the max (should use adjacent bars), incorrect initialization, and off-by-one errors in loops. Real code pattern: n = len(height); left_max = [0]*n; for i in range(1,n): left_max[i] = max(left_max[i-1], height[i-1]); right_max = [0]*n; for i in range(n-2,-1,-1): right_max[i] = max(right_max[i+1], height[i+1]). This sub-problem can be coded independently by taking height array as input.

Right_max: [4,4,4,4,0].

ladders.title: Building Block — Finding Maximum in a Subarray
ladders.desc: Compute the maximum height in a contiguous subarray of bars.
ladders.explanation: The intuition is that to determine how much water a bar can hold, we need to know the tallest bar to its left and right, which are essentially the maximum values in subarrays. This building block involves scanning a list to find the maximum value within a given range [start, end]. In the DP approach, this is optimized by cumulative updates, but understanding the basic operation is key. Step-by-step: Initialize max_val = -infinity. For each index i from start to end inclusive: if height[i] > max_val, update max_val = height[i]. Return max_val. Time complexity is O(k) where k is the subarray length, space is O(1). This connects to L2 as the naive way to compute left/right maxes, which DP improves upon. Common mistakes include incorrect range boundaries (off-by-one), not handling empty subarrays, and using min instead of max. Real code pattern: def max_in_subarray(height, start, end): max_val = float('-inf'); for i in range(start, end+1): if height[i] > max_val: max_val = height[i]; return max_val. This sub-problem can be coded independently with array and indices as input.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Comparing and Updating Maximum
ladders.desc: Compare two heights and update a running maximum value.
ladders.explanation: The intuition is that to find the maximum in a sequence, we maintain a current maximum and update it when we encounter a larger value. This is a fundamental operation used repeatedly in the DP passes. Step-by-step: Given current_max and new_height, if new_height > current_max, set current_max = new_height; otherwise, keep current_max unchanged. This operation is O(1) in time and space. It connects to L3 as the core step in scanning a subarray for its maximum. Common mistakes include using < instead of >, not initializing current_max properly (e.g., to 0 when heights could be negative—though in this problem heights are non-negative), or updating incorrectly. Real code pattern: if new_height > current_max: current_max = new_height. This sub-problem can be coded independently with two numbers as input.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Array Indexing and Loops
ladders.desc: Access elements in an array using indices and iterate through them with loops.
ladders.explanation: The intuition is that an array is a collection of elements stored in contiguous memory locations, and each element can be accessed by its index (position). In programming, indices typically start at 0. Loops allow us to traverse the array to perform operations on each element. This is foundational because the DP approach requires accessing height values at specific indices and iterating forward and backward. Step-by-step: For an array 'height' of length n, height[i] gives the element at index i, where i ranges from 0 to n-1. A forward loop: for i in range(n): access height[i]. A backward loop: for i in range(n-1, -1, -1): access height[i]. Time complexity depends on loop length, space is O(1) for indexing. This connects to L4 as we use indices to get heights for comparison. Common mistakes include index out-of-bounds errors (e.g., accessing height[n]), confusing 0-based indexing, and incorrect loop ranges. Real code pattern: n = len(height); for i in range(n): print(height[i]). This sub-problem can be coded independently by creating a simple array and looping.

approaches.name: Two Pointer O(n)
approaches.summary: Use two pointers at the ends of the array, tracking the maximum heights from left and right. Move the pointer with the smaller maximum, calculating trapped water based on the current maximum, which allows in-place computation without extra arrays.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Two Pointer Trapping Rain Water
ladders.desc: Complete solution using two pointers to compute trapped rainwater in O(n) time and O(1) space.
ladders.explanation: The two-pointer approach solves the trapping rain water problem efficiently by maintaining two pointers at the ends of the elevation array and tracking the maximum heights encountered from both sides. The intuition is that water trapped at any position depends on the minimum of the maximum heights to its left and right, minus the height at that position. By starting from both ends and moving inward, we can compute this without extra arrays. We initialize left pointer at index 0, right pointer at last index, left_max as height[0], right_max as height[n-1], and total_water = 0. While left <= right, we compare left_max and right_max. If left_max <= right_max, we process the left side: calculate water at left as max(0, left_max - height[left]), add to total, update left_max if height[left] is larger, and move left pointer right. Otherwise, process the right side similarly with right_max. This works because moving the pointer with the smaller maximum ensures we always know the limiting height for water trapping. Time complexity is O(n) as we traverse each element once, and space is O(1) using only a few variables. This feeds directly from L1 by implementing the full algorithm. Common mistakes include not updating maxima correctly or missing the condition for moving pointers. Real code pattern: def trap(height): n = len(height); if n < 3: return 0; left, right = 0, n-1; left_max, right_max = height[left], height[right]; total = 0; while left <= right: if left_max <= right_max: water = max(0, left_max - height[left]); total += water; left_max = max(left_max, height[left]); left += 1 else: water = max(0, right_max - height[right]); total += water; right_max = max(right_max, height[right]); right -= 1; return total.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Two Pointer Water Calculation Loop
ladders.desc: Implement the core loop that moves pointers and calculates trapped water based on current maxima.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the heart of the two-pointer approach, where we iterate through the elevation array using left and right pointers to compute trapped water without extra space. The intuition is that by maintaining left_max and right_max, we can determine the water level at the current pointer positions. We start with pointers at both ends and their corresponding maxima. In each iteration, we compare left_max and right_max. If left_max is smaller or equal, we know the water trapped at the left pointer is limited by left_max (since right_max is at least as large), so we calculate water as max(0, left_max - height[left]), add to total, update left_max if the current height is larger, and move the left pointer right. Otherwise, we do the same for the right side. This ensures we always process the side with the smaller maximum, which is safe because the larger maximum guarantees water trapping potential. The algorithm runs in O(n) time as each pointer moves at most n steps, and space is O(1). This feeds into L0 by providing the main loop for the full solution. Common mistakes include incorrect pointer movement or not handling negative water values. Real code pattern: while left <= right: if left_max <= right_max: water = max(0, left_max - height[left]); total += water; left_max = max(left_max, height[left]); left += 1 else: water = max(0, right_max - height[right]); total += water; right_max = max(right_max, height[right]); right -= 1.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Minimum of Maxima Water Trapping
ladders.desc: Understand that water trapped at any point equals min(left_max, right_max) - height[i], if positive.
ladders.explanation: This core logic is the fundamental insight for solving trapping rain water: at any index i, the amount of water that can be trapped is determined by the minimum of the maximum heights to its left and right, minus the height at i, but only if this value is positive. Intuitively, water will fill up to the level of the shorter 'wall' on either side, and any excess height at i reduces the trapped water. For example, if left_max is 5 and right_max is 3 at index i with height 1, water trapped is min(5,3) - 1 = 3 - 1 = 2. If height at i is 4, then min(5,3) - 4 = -1, so no water is trapped (we take max with 0). This works because water cannot exceed the lower barrier. In the two-pointer approach, we use this logic implicitly by comparing left_max and right_max to decide which side to process. Time and space are O(1) for a single calculation. This feeds into L1 by providing the formula used in the loop. Common mistakes include forgetting to take the minimum or not handling negative results. Real code pattern: water = max(0, min(left_max, right_max) - height[i]).

ladders.title: Building Block — Tracking Maximum Heights with Pointers
ladders.desc: Maintain running maximums from left and right while traversing an array with two pointers.
ladders.explanation: This building block involves using two pointers to traverse an array from both ends while keeping track of the maximum values encountered so far from each side. The intuition is that as we move inward, we update left_max to be the highest height seen from the left up to the current left pointer, and similarly for right_max from the right. This allows us to know, at any point, the maximum heights on either side without storing entire arrays. For example, starting with left at index 0 and right at last index, we set left_max = height[0] and right_max = height[n-1]. As we move left pointer right, if we encounter a taller height, we update left_max; similarly for the right pointer moving left. This is efficient because it uses O(1) space and O(n) time. It feeds into L2 by providing the left_max and right_max values needed for the water calculation. Common mistakes include not initializing maxima correctly or updating them in the wrong order. Real code pattern: left_max = max(left_max, height[left]); right_max = max(right_max, height[right]).

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Comparing and Updating Values with Max
ladders.desc: Use comparison and max operations to update running values during traversal.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation involves comparing two values and updating a variable to the maximum of itself and another value. In the context of the two-pointer approach, we frequently compare left_max and right_max to decide which pointer to move, and we update left_max or right_max when we encounter a taller height. The intuition is simple: we want to keep track of the highest value seen so far from each side. For example, if left_max is 3 and we see height[left] = 5, we update left_max to 5. Similarly, we compare left_max and right_max to determine the smaller one, which dictates the water trapping potential. This operation is fundamental because it enables efficient tracking without extra data structures. It runs in O(1) time and space. This feeds into L3 by being the mechanism for maintaining maxima. Common mistakes include using min instead of max or not updating correctly. Real code pattern: if a > b: update; else: update; or use max() function.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Arrays and Pointers
ladders.desc: Learn basic array traversal and pointer manipulation for accessing elements.
ladders.explanation: This foundation concept covers the basics of arrays and pointers, which are essential for the two-pointer approach. An array is a collection of elements stored in contiguous memory, accessible by indices starting from 0. Pointers are variables that store indices or references to positions in the array. In this context, we use two integer pointers, 'left' and 'right', to represent positions in the array. Initially, left points to the first element (index 0) and right points to the last element (index n-1, where n is the array length). We can access elements using array indexing, e.g., height[left] gives the value at the left pointer. As we traverse, we move left pointer right by incrementing it (left += 1) and move right pointer left by decrementing it (right -= 1). This allows us to examine each element without extra space. The intuition is that by starting from both ends, we can efficiently process the array. Time complexity for traversal is O(n), and space is O(1). This feeds into L4 by providing the structure for comparison and updates. Common mistakes include going out of bounds or confusing pointer movements. Real code pattern: left = 0; right = len(height) - 1; while left <= right: access height[left] and height[right]; update pointers.

approaches.name: Stack-Based O(n)
approaches.summary: Use a monotonic decreasing stack to process bars, popping when a higher bar is found and calculating trapped water for the bounded area. This method handles the problem by considering local minima and is useful for related problems like largest rectangle in histogram.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Stack-Based Trapping Rain Water
ladders.desc: Complete solution using monotonic decreasing stack to compute trapped rainwater between elevation bars.
ladders.explanation: The stack-based approach solves the trapping rain water problem by processing each bar once and using a stack to track bars in decreasing height order. The intuition is that water gets trapped between two higher bars with a lower bar in between, forming a bounded area. We maintain a monotonic decreasing stack (strictly decreasing heights) as we iterate through the array. When we encounter a bar taller than the top of the stack, it means we've found a right boundary that can trap water with a left boundary (the next bar in the stack). We pop the top (which represents the bottom of the potential water trap) and calculate the water trapped between the new top (left boundary) and current bar (right boundary). The water height is determined by the minimum of left and right boundaries minus the popped bar's height, and the width is the distance between boundaries. This process continues while the current bar is taller than the stack top, ensuring we calculate all trapped water for that region. Time complexity is O(n) as each bar is pushed and popped at most once, and space is O(n) for the stack in worst case (strictly decreasing heights). Connection to L1: This integrates the key subroutine of processing a bar with the stack. Common mistakes include incorrect width calculation (should be distance between indices minus 1) and not handling empty stack properly. Real code pattern: Initialize total=0, stack=[]. For i, h in enumerate(heights): while stack and h > heights[stack[-1]]: pop top, if stack empty break, left=stack[-1], height=min(h, heights[left])-heights[top], width=i-left-1, total+=height*width. Push i to stack. Return total.

ladders.title: Key Sub-Routine — Process Bar with Stack for Water Calculation
ladders.desc: Handle a single bar's interaction with the stack to compute trapped water when a higher bar is found.
ladders.explanation: This subroutine is the core of the stack-based approach, where we process each bar index i with height h against a monotonic decreasing stack. The intuition is that when we encounter a bar taller than the top of the stack, it acts as a right boundary that can trap water with a left boundary (the previous bar in the stack). We repeatedly pop from the stack while h > height of stack top, calculating water for each popped bar. For each pop: let top = stack.pop(), if stack is empty break (no left boundary), left = stack[-1] (left boundary index), trapped height = min(h, height[left]) - height[top] (water level minus bottom height), width = i - left - 1 (distance between boundaries), add height * width to total. This computes water trapped between left and current bar with top as the bottom. After popping all lower bars, push i onto stack. This ensures stack remains monotonic decreasing. Time per bar is amortized O(1) due to at most one push and pop per bar, space O(n) for stack. Connection to L0: This subroutine is called for each bar in the full solution. Common mistakes: forgetting to check stack empty after pop, incorrect width calculation (use i-left-1 not i-top). Real code pattern: def process_bar(i, h, stack, heights, total): while stack and h > heights[stack[-1]]: top = stack.pop(); if not stack: break; left = stack[-1]; height = min(h, heights[left]) - heights[top]; width = i - left - 1; total += height * width. stack.append(i).

ladders.title: Core Logic — Monotonic Decreasing Stack for Bounded Areas
ladders.desc: Use a stack that maintains decreasing heights to identify left and right boundaries for water trapping.
ladders.explanation: The core insight is that a monotonic decreasing stack (where heights decrease from bottom to top) helps find pairs of left and right boundaries that trap water. As we iterate through bars, we push indices onto the stack if the current height is less than or equal to the stack top's height, maintaining decreasing order. When we find a bar taller than the stack top, it becomes a right boundary. We then pop from the stack: each popped bar represents a bottom between the left boundary (new stack top after pop) and right boundary (current bar). The stack ensures that left boundaries are always higher than or equal to subsequent bars until a taller right boundary appears. This logic efficiently captures all trapped water by processing each bar once. Intuition: Water is trapped in valleys between peaks; the stack tracks potential left peaks and processes valleys when a right peak is found. Time O(n) as each index pushed/popped once, space O(n). Connection to L1: This logic is implemented in the process_bar subroutine. Common mistakes: using increasing stack (which doesn't work for this problem) or not maintaining strict decreasing order. Real code pattern: stack = []; for i, h in enumerate(heights): while stack and h > heights[stack[-1]]: # process pop. stack.append(i).

ladders.title: Building Block — Calculate Water Between Two Boundaries
ladders.desc: Compute trapped water volume between a left and right boundary with a given bottom height.
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept calculates the water trapped between two vertical boundaries (left and right bars) with a bottom bar in between. Given left boundary index L with height H_L, right boundary index R with height H_R, and bottom index B with height H_B (where L < B < R), the trapped water is (min(H_L, H_R) - H_B) * (R - L - 1). Intuition: Water level is determined by the shorter boundary (like a container), and we subtract the bottom height to get water depth. Width is the distance between boundaries excluding the boundaries themselves. This formula works because water fills the rectangular area between L and R up to the lower boundary height, minus the space occupied by the bottom bar. Time O(1), space O(1). Connection to L2: This calculation is performed each time we pop from the stack in the core logic. Common mistakes: using max instead of min, incorrect width (e.g., R-B instead of R-L-1). Real code pattern: def water_between(L, R, B, heights): height = min(heights[L], heights[R]) - heights[B]; width = R - L - 1; return height * width if height > 0 else 0.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Maintain Monotonic Stack by Popping Lower Elements
ladders.desc: Pop elements from a stack while a condition holds to keep it monotonic.
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation involves repeatedly removing elements from the top of a stack as long as a certain condition is true, typically to maintain a specific order (like monotonic decreasing). In the context of trapping rain water, we pop while the current height is greater than the height at the stack top. The algorithm: while stack is not empty and current_height > heights[stack[-1]]: pop the top. This ensures that after popping, the stack top is greater than or equal to the current height, preserving decreasing order when we push the current index. Intuition: We remove smaller bars that are 'shadowed' by the current taller bar, as they won't be needed for future water calculations. Time O(k) where k is number of pops, but amortized O(1) per element. Space O(1) extra. Connection to L3: This operation is used before calculating water between boundaries. Common mistakes: infinite loop if condition doesn't change, or popping too many elements. Real code pattern: while stack and current_height > heights[stack[-1]]: stack.pop().

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Stack Data Structure and Basic Operations
ladders.desc: Understand stack LIFO behavior and push/pop operations.
ladders.explanation: A stack is a linear data structure that follows Last-In-First-Out (LIFO) principle, meaning the last element added is the first one removed. It supports two primary operations: push (add an element to the top) and pop (remove the top element). Access is typically limited to the top element via peek or top operation. Intuition: Think of a stack of plates; you add and remove from the top. In programming, stacks are used for function calls, undo operations, and parsing expressions. For trapping rain water, we use a stack to store indices of bars in decreasing height order. Time: push and pop are O(1) operations. Space: O(n) where n is number of elements stored. Connection to L4: This foundation enables the monotonic stack operations. Common mistakes: confusing stack with queue (FIFO), or trying to access middle elements directly. Real code pattern: stack = [] (list as stack in Python), stack.append(item) for push, item = stack.pop() for pop, if stack: top = stack[-1] for peek.
